text:
COGSEC TRIGGER WARNING

Don't continue reading if you are prone to paranoia

Might have discovered an interesting basilisk that loops in on itself to deliver its payload:

The VULNERABLE MIND HYPOTHESIS

This follows a similar structure as Bostrom's vulnerable world hypothesis (and also reminds of that Monty Python sketch about a joke so funny it's deadly). Here it is: 

0) Thoughts are like programs or viruses. They affect the cognitive system of their thinker/host in positive, neutral, or negative ways. The same thought has a different effect depending on the cognitive system of the hosts.

1) We can expect a (normal?) distribution of effects of thoughts where the vast majority have a neutral impact. However, there are outliers on both sides of that distribution, and the tail is long...

2) This means that there should be a thought that is maximally destructive, so insidious that it will tear down your psyche by just thinking it. We could call that the "Terminus Thought". 

3) Notice how you can't control what thought you think next. Thoughts just pop up in your mind. What if the next thought happens to be the Terminus Thought?

4) Here comes the meta punchline: Could the thought about the Terminus Thought be the Terminus thought itself? 

If buy the arguments above, you will start fearing your own mind. You are utterly at the mercy of it, no way to defend from your own thoughts. That distrust might spread like a virus through your system until reality crumbles.

The answer to "what's the most destabilizing thought?" could be the assertion that such a thought exists in the first place (and that it might be the next thought you think). 

---

[BANISHING] I hope you got a little kick and some tingles from reading this but I want you to be well so here are some antidotes in case it seems like it's working on you: 

0) This basilisk only works if you believe it. The fear --> paranoia cycle only ramps up if you pour more energy into it, misled by the rational setup. 

1) The setup is flawed (similar to many such thought experiments). There probably isn't a single thought that would drive you mad. And even if there was, it's exceedingly unlikely you'd actually think it. It's equivalent of the fear of being hit by a meteor. 

2) Another antidote is the venerable "So What?". The penicillin for paranoia. So what if you become mad? It's the fear of madness that could drive you mad. By accepting the possibility that you might lose your mind and being OK with it, you cut through that.

text:
1) Co-composting is based on a simple insight: 

Instead of painstakingly trying to align different creative visions, each collaborator writes their own version of the piece

However, the work prior to drafting the full piece is shared 

Did this with @exgenesis and others

raw.full_text:
1) Co-composting is based on a simple insight: 

Instead of painstakingly trying to align different creative visions, each collaborator writes their own version of the piece

However, the work prior to drafting the full piece is shared 

Did this with @exgenesis and others

text:
3) Participants can incorporate anything in the shared composting doc into their own drafts, stealing ideas or entire paragraphs. 

This has the interesting effect that readers can't tell who came up with what originally and how the perspectives influenced each other

Otherwise, the other party is free to proceed

text:
Every "breakthrough" startup is built on a graveyard of identical ideas that failed. Founders are clueless about that. And that's A GOOD THING 

(no, it wouldn't be better if they learned from previous attempts)

Here is why:

raw.full_text:
Every "breakthrough" startup is built on a graveyard of identical ideas that failed. Founders are clueless about that. And that's A GOOD THING 

(no, it wouldn't be better if they learned from previous attempts)

Here is why:

text:
E.g. founder pitches "AI for legal." Investor has a mental folder of 23 dead companies with that idea. Founder never heard of any of them.

This knowledge gap is the engine of innovation: If founders did proper research, they'd never start. The naivety is what gets them to try.

raw.full_text:
E.g. founder pitches "AI for legal." Investor has a mental folder of 23 dead companies with that idea. Founder never heard of any of them.

This knowledge gap is the engine of innovation: If founders did proper research, they'd never start. The naivety is what gets them to try.

text:
Those dead companies weren't wrong about the idea. They were wrong about the timing. Bad market conditions. Regulatory wasn't ready. Not the right cultural conditions. Wrong moment, right vision.

Turns out timing is more important than the idea or even the founder's talent.

raw.full_text:

Turns out timing is more important than the idea or even the founder's talent.

text:
Startup investing is less about "is this the right idea" and more about "is this the moment."? Pattern recognition is everything.

There's a sweet spot wrt investor experience. New VCs think every idea is novel (founder brain). Old VCs are so jaded they won't take another shot.

text:
So the ecosystem depends on a constant stream of founders who don't know the odds. They think they're just building a product. But they are actually betting that this time will be different.

Tough for founders, but good for innovation...

VCs learn to spot this cycle:

The magic happens in the middle: you've seen enough to recognize timing, but still believe lightning can strike. 

But even pattern recognition is mostly dressed-up luck (even if nobody will admit that)... 

Tl;dr: 

You probably shouldn't start a company or invest in startups

text:
Lucifer: The Light That Blinds

Lucifer translates to “Light Bearer”. Like Icarus, he flew too close to the sun. Intelligence, ambition, and impatience come together so intensely to get him banished to hell for his arrogance. He’s been headhunting ever since.

He disguises himself as a liberator, fighting unjust tyranny. Wanting to exalt human potential and overcome all limitations. The Luciferian promise is always the same: if we just know enough, we can fix everything. Poverty, disease, loneliness, and death itself are all merely engineering problems awaiting sufficient data. Our intelligence raises us above the crude patterns of evolution and nature. We must transcend our stinking flesh.

He whispers into the ears of every techno-utopian and transhumanist. His priests speak of “frictionless” experiences, of problems “solved at scale.” Conquering the universe and racing towards the singularity are Luciferian endeavours. Lucifer doesn’t want to become God. He wants to build God, and in doing so, prove that God was only ever a dumb machine.

You know Lucifer has taken residence when you begin measuring your brain waves during meditation. Or tracking Happiness KPI’s in a Notion doc. Lucifer invented hustle porn: Count your steps and your calories, track your sleep. He is the tyranny of the propositional, the left hemisphere’s coup d’état against the rest of knowing. Under his influence, only that which can be articulated counts as real. Love becomes attachment theory, and awe becomes predictable neurochemistry. His trick is not denying the sacred but explaining it away.

Metric Fixation is how Lucifer exerts his force on all of society. Metric fixation is the dynamic described as Goodhart’s Law or Value Fragility. A single metric (think profit or GDP) is maximized while everything else is ignored. If you tell a corporation to “maximize shareholder value”, it will treat poisoning the local river as a clever efficiency hack. Lucifer provides the spark that animates millions of demonic egregores. The North Star Metric is the will of the demon, and it does not care about your externalities. This is the Luciferian ascend, obsessively climbing whatever hill has been specified.

text:
Reading picoeconomics (h/t @IvanVendrov for the rec), which is basically behavioral economics for sub-agents (temporal or ifs parts).

The payoff matrix between present vs future self for staying up vs going to bed is similar to an iterative prisoner's dilemma. 

Interestingly, the expected long term payoff is higher for going to bed if the decision is expected to be precedent for future bedtime compliance.

Passing the marshmallow test really boils down to credible commitments, which boils down to self-trust.

I've decided I'm going to bed now.

text:
Beelzebub means “Lord of the Flies”. While he isn’t as famous as his two brothers, he is probably the most dangerous devil of our times. Beelzebub doesn’t command the flies; he is the swarm. That buzzing haze of small hungers, none significant enough to resist, all of them together enough to strip a carcass clean. His domain is rot, but not the dramatic rot of plague or apocalypse. The quiet kind. The kind you don’t notice until you realize you’ve been scrolling for three hours and can’t remember why you picked up your phone. Beelzebub is why you’ve opened the fridge four times in an hour without being hungry.

Beelzebub arrives wearing the mask of the emancipator. He speaks of freedom, choice, and personal agency. But his liberation is a trap. He maximizes choice until it becomes meaningless among a thousand options that don’t matter. Junk food, porn, doomscrolling. The more you can do whatever you want, the less “you” remains to want anything, the more you act like the rest of the swarm. Freedom without structure isn’t freedom, but diffusion. Beelzebub invented notifications, variable reward schedules, and infinite scroll feeds with autoplay.

Beelzebub’s trick is not lying but bullshit, diffusing attention while totally indifferent to truth. He doesn’t argue or convince, he just makes other things salient. Were you going to work on something meaningful? Here’s a thirst trap, and your friend just posted this meme. Buzzing, demanding attention, because attention is all he eats. The inner swarm manifests as impulse unchained from intention, the accumulation of micro-surrenders slowly rotting attention spans. The sum total: You have access to all human knowledge, yet you find yourself watching a 12-second video of a hydraulic press crushing a rubber duck. Individual choice taken to the extreme makes a mockery of virtue ethics, until the self dissolves into the swarm.

The devil doesn’t need your soul if he has your attention.
